dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems. Patient is at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY]. She is showing signs of altered mental status.
Hi, my name is Morgan, I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
We came in this morning and found her on the couch. She's having trouble breathing and not acting like herself.
Is the breathing problem the exact issue that prompted you to call 911?
That's right. Her lips were turning blue when we first saw her.
On a scale from -5 to +5, she's currently unresponsive to verbal stimuli but localizes to pain.
Can you tell me whether her eyes are open when not distracted?
Yes, they are open normally, but she doesn't seem focused.
Does she seem to respond to other people in the house?
She doesn't talk at all. I've been calling her name, but she just ignores it.
Does she move when she's touched or is touched by someone else?
Yes. Once I pinched her hand, she moved it slightly, though it felt odd.
We're going to get a general sense of her breathing and airway. Can we take a good look at her?
Yes, go ahead. She's been sitting up the whole time.
Her airway is patent and self-maintained. She is not showing signs of obstructive breathing.
Ma'am, we are going to take a peak at your breathing. We'll check a few things to be safe.
We are noting fast breathing at 24 breaths per minute with bilateral rales.
RR is 24.
Ma'am, we are going to take your pulse and blood pressure now.
Her radial pulse is strong and regular. Heart rate is 94.
Blood pressure is 138 over 98.
SpO₂ is 96 on room air.
We need to go over her medical history. Were you aware of any conditions she has had?
She has had a few strokes before, I think-never sure of the exact term. She was on blood thinners at one point.
Is there anything she is allergic to?
I don't think so. I've never noticed any adverse reactions she's had.
Any medications she is on currently you're aware of?
We have an old medication bottle nearby, but can't read what it says clearly.
When was the last time she had something to eat or drink-like breakfast, coffee, juice?
We woke up at 9:15 a.m. I gave her an orange juice, but she didn't drink it.
When do you think she started to feel unwell? Was it earlier than that?
No. She looked fine when we went to bed about 11:30 p.m. Woke up and she was like this.
Does she appear to experience any pain today?
I think she doesn't feel any pain-that's what I'm really worried about. She's just quiet.
Her skin feels warm, dry, and pink. May we check the full surface for rashes or bruises?
You go ahead. If that can help, then sure.
No indications of bruising or rash. Skin appears normal, and no bite marks are visible.
She has right-sided facial droop. Can I look a bit closer?
Feel free to check.
We are noting a pacemaker on the left chest. Can we listen to her lungs and heart?
Yes, please.
Fluid sounds in the lungs bilaterally. Heart appears regular given the pacemaker, no irregular sounds.
Blood pressure at 142 over 105.
Heart rate is now 91.
SpO₂ has improved to 99.
Respiratory rate remains 20 breaths per minute.
We're about to start an IV to get fluids ready. Can you confirm the inside of her arms is open for insertion?
She's got a good left arm that I can assist with.
IV successfully established on the left AC site with an 18-gauge needle.
Normal saline administered and flushed through successfully.
Glucose is at 107 mg/dL.
Sinus arrhythmia noted in 4-lead.
She fits the Stroke/TIA protocol. Please activate the stroke alert.
We are proceeding with lights and sirens to [COMPANY_NAME] with a Stroke Alert.
I'll handle the radio communication while you do a quick second check.
Blood pressure now at 149 over 89.
Radial pulse is 78 now, stable.
SpO₂ is 98.
Preparing to scan EKG with 12 lead. Let's record and recheck heart condition.
12-lead is abnormal, with left atrial enlargements noted and likely minimal LVH.
Also frequent ventricular premature complexes noted at this time.
She has a slight weight on her right side. Need your help turning her just to lift her from the couch.
She's secured with 5 straps and 2 railings. Safety check complete.
She's safely loaded in the ambulance. Let's move her with sirens on now.
[COMPANY_NAME] staff, this is Morgan with Unit 3. She is ready for you. Patient is showing stroke symptoms with altered mental status and paced heart.
Her BP 149 over 89, SpO₂ 98, heart rate 78, and normal airway, with a secure IV.
Stroke alert protocol is confirmed. Vitals are stable but EKG shows significant LAV and PVCs.
Described prior strokes and wake-up issue at 0915 hours with no known medications or allergies recorded.
Complete report and signed document transferred to your nurse, as observed on arrival and in transit.
Right-sided facial droop was noted, further supporting the field impression of acute cerebrovascular accident.
Prior strokes were noted by the husband, suggesting possible recurrence risk.
Awareness of pain and reactivity remained unchanged throughout the transport.
She remains leaning to the right side while secured on the stretcher.
Her respiratory rate is still at 20, with no signs of distress.
Later vitals showed a BP of 133/82 and SpO₂ of 99.
Final 12-lead EKG repeated showed nonspecific intraventricular conduction block with QRS duration of 130+ ms.
Her positioning was again reviewed. She remains stable on a 2-rail secured cot.
Final BP of 133/82, still within acceptable range for transport.
Last glucose reading was still within normal limits-107 mg/dL.
Airway remains patent and unobstructed throughout the event.
No change in respirations, still at 24 per minute with bilateral rales.
She remains unresponsive to verbal stimuli and only reacts minimally to pain. Her GCS score is 10 (Eyes - 4, Verbal - 1, Motor - 5).
Peripheral pulses remain strong and equal.
She is currently loaded and secured for transport. Jaundice was not noted.
She has been transported safely with a clear communication of stroke protocol to the receiving hospital.
No limb weakness was observed, based on her positioning and lack of limb use.
No unexpected changes in vitals occurred during the transport-stable systolic and diastolic readings.
Stroke alert was activated properly. Hospital response was timely and in line with acute stroke management.
All critical neurological findings were documented and verbally transferred.
No further interventions were required during the transport phase.
She presents no signs of shock or hypoperfusion, and no additional oxygen or advanced airway management was required.
Her skin turgor was not assessed due to patient's unresponsiveness. Signs of dehydration at this time were not identified.
Transport was completed in under 30 minutes via sirens, minimizing the time to hospital arrival for CT and stroke protocol activation.
Final reassessment confirms her stable status: BP 133/82, HR 78, RR 20, and SpO₂ 99.
All EMS actions were performed with the goal of rapid neurological evaluation and stroke-specific care by the receiving center.
The patient was properly identified and handed over to the care of [COMPANY_NAME] after a full report discussion with hospital staff.
Particular effort was made to facilitate immediate hospital action with alerts for stroke.
The absence of verbal or visible signs of distress does not contradict the field impression of acute stroke.
Pupillary size was not assessed due to unresponsiveness. No obvious signs of nuchal rigidity detected.
All documentation includes time stamps for procedures and findings.
Continuation of vitals verified the field impression is accurate.
No confusion was present in the reported condition between the field report and the call to the receiving hospital.
EMS did not delay care and acted under optimal timeframes for stroke response.
An appropriate time window for stroke care was communicated to hospital staff.
Named nurse received handover and signed EMT form upon transfer.
Our intervention goal was to get the patient to an appropriate facility quickly and safely for stroke evaluation.
All performance met field EMS standards for acute neurological handling.
